frameworks.name: Attention-to-Perception Bridge
frameworks.definition: Changing what you focus attention on (the frame or narrative) restructures how you perceive a situation, which restructures your emotional and behavioral response.

claims.text: Reframing a situation by changing which attributes you attend to — emphasizing positive aspects rather than negative ones — produces a different perception and emotional response to the same objective reality.
claims.speaker: Unknown
claims.mechanism: When attention shifts to different aspects of an experience (from 'bus = poor alternative' to 'bus = convenient direct route'), the emotional valence and behavioral response shift accordingly.

examples.text: On an airplane about to land, passengers universally perceive a bus connection negatively, viewing it as the 'poor man's air bridge' or 'crappy way' to reach the terminal. A pilot restructures the framing by naming two specific attributes: (1) the unavoidable constraint (airbridge blocked by another plane) and (2) the positive aspect of the bus (goes all the way to passport control, requiring no further walking with carry-on bags). By redirecting passenger attention from 'bus as substandard' to 'bus as convenience,' the pilot changed perception and emotional response to the same bus ride without changing the objective reality of the bus itself.
